history_of_present_illness.definition: Deep Inferior Epigastric Perforator flap - a breast reconstruction that uses skin and fat from your lower tummy, without taking muscle, to rebuild the breast.
An operation to remove breast tissue. Yours was skin-sparing, which keeps the outer skin to help reconstruction.
The place tissue was taken from - here, your lower abdomen. It heals over the coming weeks and feels tight at first.
A soft tube that lets extra fluid drain out of the wound while it heals. The nurse removes it once the output is low.

physical_exam.definition: A quick check of blood flow - pressing the skin and watching the colour return. Brisk return means the flap has a healthy blood supply.
A collection of blood under the skin. Sudden severe swelling or pressure at a wound can signal this and needs urgent review.
A pocket of clear fluid that can gather under a healing wound. Small ones often settle on their own.

assessment.definition: Means blood is flowing well through the tissue. A well-perfused flap is a healthy flap.
When a wound edge starts to open or separate. This is one of the signs to report to the practice.
A lower-than-normal level of red blood cells or haemoglobin. Mild anaemia is common after major surgery and is monitored.

plan.definition: An antibiotic given for a short course after surgery to help prevent wound infection.
A blood-thinning injection that lowers the risk of clots (DVT) while you are less mobile.
A regular pain reliever (Panadol). Taking it around the clock keeps pain steady and reduces the need for stronger medicine.
